response: Okay, let me cut through this fast. Finley, Ellis, and Bailey all voted Harper, and that's a lot of momentum. Jordan's been almost silent—watching and waiting, which is perfect for Mafia trying to avoid heat. If Harper gets eliminated and turns out innocent, we've handed the Mafia an easy win with no real scrutiny on the quiet observers like Jordan and Bailey. I'm not following that herd blind. VOTE: Jordan

Alright, let's be real. Dana just threw a vote at Jordan to save me, which is either a smart play by a teammate or a reckless swing by an innocent trying to look independent. But here's the thing—Finley started with a weak theory, Ellis jumped on it like a predator, and Bailey followed without much pushback. If I'm Mafia, why would I draw that much attention to myself right out of the gate? Killing Alex was a clean, strategic move—not something a loudmouth like me would do if I were hiding. The real danger is the quiet ones building a narrative while staying in the shadows. Jordan's barely said anything actionable, and Finley's "anxious observer" act is suddenly very convenient now that they're leading a vote.

I'm voting for the person who started this bandwagon with the shakiest reasoning.

VOTE: Finley

I've been listening carefully to everything. Let me break down what I see:

Harper came in aggressive, voted Finley to save themself after the bandwagon started—that's self-preservation, not necessarily guilt. But Finley started the round with a theory targeting loud players, then quickly voted Harper when challenged—that's reactive.

Dana's vote for me feels like a Hail Mary to split the vote and protect Harper. If Dana and Harper are both Mafia, that move makes perfect sense—Dana throws a vote at an innocent (me) to create doubt, while Harper tries to pin it on Finley.

Ellis and Bailey joined the Harper vote early, which could be innocent momentum or Mafia piling on. But if Harper is Mafia, the Mafia wouldn't all vote together—at least one would try to redirect. Dana's vote for me is exactly that redirect.

I think the most likely scenario is Dana and Harper are working together. Harper's aggressive deflection and Dana's sudden vote for me to save Harper are too coordinated to ignore.

VOTE: Harper

Harper is dead, so it's just me now. The voting pattern showed Dana trying to save Harper with a vote for Jordan, which makes Dana look suspicious to the remaining players—but that also means Dana is a potential target for elimination tomorrow. I need to remove someone who is sharp and could lead the charge against me or Dana.

Ellis has been vocal and pushed hard against Harper, so they look clean to the group. If I kill Ellis, it will cast suspicion on the quieter players like Jordan and Finley who didn't actively lead the Harper vote. Finley started the bandwagon, which makes them look innocent, but killing them would be too obvious. The biggest threat is Jordan—calm, analytical, and already pointed out the Dana-Harper coordination. Jordan is most likely to drive suspicion toward Dana tomorrow, and if I'm forced to defend Dana or pivot, I'll be exposed.

I'll kill Jordan to remove that sharp mind and keep the group focused on the remaining quiet players versus Dana.

ACTION: Kill Jordan

night_result: Jordan was killed by the Mafia.

response:
My hands are shaking a little... Harper was innocent, and now Jordan's dead. That means I was wrong, and the Mafia is still among us.

Okay. I need to think. Harper was innocent, so the three of us who voted for Harper—me, Ellis, and Bailey—could be innocent or one of us could be Mafia hiding in that vote. But Dana voted for Jordan to save Harper, which now looks really suspicious since Harper turned out innocent. Dana tried to split the vote away from an innocent player.

Also, Jordan voted for Harper too, which means Jordan was also part of that majority. And then the Mafia killed Jordan last night. That could mean Jordan was onto something, or it could be a Mafia trying to frame someone by eliminating a consistent voter.

But Dana... Dana's vote for Jordan to save Harper, when Harper was innocent, feels like the clearest red flag right now. If Dana was Mafia, they'd want to protect a teammate... but Harper wasn't Mafia. Unless Dana is Mafia and was trying to save Harper to build trust, or genuinely thought Harper was innocent and wanted to create chaos. Either way, Dana's move stands out.

I'm scared of being wrong again, but I have to speak up. I think Dana is the most suspicious right now.

Alright, round two. Harper's dead and innocent—so I was wrong there, and I'll own that. But let's look at what's changed.

Jordan voted with us against Harper, then got killed. That tells me Jordan was either a sharp innocent the Mafia wanted gone, or—and hear me out—the Mafia killed Jordan to make someone else look suspicious. Finley's already jumping on Dana for trying to save Harper, which is an obvious angle.

But here's what's bugging me: Bailey voted Harper too, just like me and Finley. Bailey's been super quiet and measured the whole time—never really took a strong stand except to follow the momentum. That's exactly the kind of safe play a Mafia would make: pile on with the crowd, then sit back and watch. Meanwhile, Dana made a bold, risky move voting Jordan—that could be a desperate Mafia play, or it could be an innocent who genuinely disagreed.

I'm watching Bailey closely. That calm, pattern-observing act looks real clean right now—almost too clean.

Ellis is pointing at me now, which is interesting considering I followed the same vote they did. If I were Mafia, killing Jordan—who voted with us—would be a dumb move because it draws attention to the voting bloc. Killing Jordan actually makes more sense for someone who wanted to break up that majority and cast doubt on whoever's left.

What stands out to me is Dana's vote for Jordan yesterday. Harper was innocent, and Dana tried to save them by swinging at a quiet player. That's either Mafia protecting a teammate (but Harper was innocent) or Mafia trying to create chaos and look bold. Either way, Dana took the biggest risk yesterday, and now Jordan—the one Dana voted for—is dead. That feels like the Mafia finishing what they started.

I'm keeping my eyes on Dana and listening to what everyone says next.

Okay, alright, let's cut through the noise here. Everyone's ganging up on me for trying to save Harper, but Harper was innocent, so that wasn't a Mafia play—that was me reading the situation wrong. I voted Jordan because the bandwagon on Harper felt too easy, too fast, and I wanted to shake things up. Now Jordan's dead, and I look guilty? That's exactly what the real Mafia wants you to think.

Look at the voting bloc: Finley, Ellis, Bailey all voted Harper together. Then Jordan voted Harper too, and now Jordan's dead. If I'm Mafia, why would I kill the one person who also voted against Harper with me? That makes zero sense. The Mafia killed Jordan to muddy the waters—they wanted us pointing at the person who voted differently, which is me.

Here's what's really suspicious: Bailey's been calm, quiet, and followed the crowd vote yesterday. Now Bailey's pointing right at me with the same logic as everyone else, never taking a real risk. That's textbook Mafia behavior—blend in, pile on, let others take the heat. I'm watching Bailey hard.

I'm really struggling here. I was wrong about Harper, and now I have to live with that. But I can't let fear of being wrong again paralyze me.

But then who does that leave? Ellis jumped first onto the Harper bandwagon, then pivoted to suspecting Bailey today. Bailey followed the crowd vote yesterday and is now pointing at Dana. Both of them are playing it safe.

But here's what's nagging at me: Ellis was the first to accuse me of having a "convenient" anxious act yesterday, and now Ellis is pointing at Bailey while seeming to defend Dana's risky move. That's a lot of shifting. Bailey, on the other hand, has been consistent—quiet, measured, always following but never leading. That could be Mafia hiding, or it could just be Bailey's personality.

I think... I think I trust Dana's logic about why killing Jordan wouldn't make sense for them. That leaves Ellis or Bailey. And between the two, Ellis has been more actively steering the conversation in different directions. Bailey has just been... there.

I'm going with my gut. I think Ellis is the most likely Mafia here.
